steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence question. The researchers conclude that electric lighting (which spread in the late nineteenth century) prompted a behavioral shift — specifically, that people began engaging in more nighttime activity. The correct answer must present data from the novel analysis that would directly confirm an increase in late-night activity in novels published after electric lighting became widespread.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Novels published after the year 1800 include the clock phrase 10 a.m. less often than novels published before the year 1800 do.: 10 a.m. is a daytime hour and is unaffected by artificial lighting. A shift there has no connection to the researchers’ claim about electric lighting and nighttime behavior. ✗
  • Choice B — Novels published after 1880 contain significantly more references to activities occurring after 10 p.m. than do novels from earlier periods.: this directly supports the claim. Electric lighting spread in the late 1800s; if post-1880 novels suddenly reference far more late-night activity, that reflects the behavioral change the researchers describe. ✓
  • Choice C — Among novels published in the nineteenth century, implied time references become steadily more common than clock phrases as publication dates approach 1900.: this tracks a shift in writing style or convention, not a shift in when characters are awake or active. It does not support the behavioral conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — The time references of noon (12 p.m.) and midnight (12 a.m.) are used with roughly the same frequency in the novels.: uniform frequency across all novels shows no change over time, which would undermine rather than support the claim that behavior shifted in response to electric lighting. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
